quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Install with brew install anomalyco/tap/opencode, npm i -g opencode-ai, or the install script
Run opencode in your project directory to open the TUI
Use /connect to add a provider or sign in to OpenCode Zen, then pick a model
Press Tab to switch between build and plan agents, type @file to pull files into context, and start with a natural-language request
For repeatable scripting, use opencode run "task" instead of the TUI

dailyWorkflows.title: Interactive coding in the TUI
dailyWorkflows.description: Open the terminal UI in the current project and iterate normally.

dailyWorkflows.title: One-shot headless task
dailyWorkflows.description: Run a non-interactive coding or analysis request from scripts or shell history.
dailyWorkflows.command: opencode run "review this diff for bugs and security issues"

dailyWorkflows.title: Safe planning pass
dailyWorkflows.description: Use the read-only plan agent before allowing edits in an unfamiliar repository.
dailyWorkflows.command:
opencode
# then press Tab to switch to plan

dailyWorkflows.title: Bring files in with fuzzy references
dailyWorkflows.description: Reference files directly from the prompt without manually pasting paths or content.

dailyWorkflows.title: Manage MCP-backed tooling
dailyWorkflows.description: Register local or remote MCP servers and let the model use them like built-in tools.

powerMoves.title: Use separate runtime and TUI config
powerMoves.description: Keep opencode.json for models, permissions, tools, MCP, and agents, while using tui.json for themes, keybinds, and scrolling behavior.
powerMoves.why: Stops UI tweaks from getting mixed with server/runtime config and makes overrides easier to reason about.

powerMoves.title: Create specialized agents
powerMoves.description: Define custom primary or subagents in JSON or markdown with tailored prompts, models, tools, and permission rules.
powerMoves.why: You can keep a fast planner, a strict reviewer, or a deployment-focused agent instead of overloading one default personality.

powerMoves.title: Scope MCP servers per agent
powerMoves.description: Disable noisy MCP tools globally and only enable them for the one agent that really needs them.
powerMoves.command: # configure tools: { "my-mcp*": false } globally, then re-enable in a custom agent
powerMoves.why: Keeps context smaller and avoids expensive tool catalogs polluting every session.

powerMoves.title: Use the desktop app for richer review flows
powerMoves.description: Switch to the desktop app when you want the file tree, review panel, timeline, and deep-linkable sessions.
powerMoves.why: The desktop app is the easiest path for long sessions where terminal-only context switching gets noisy.

powerMoves.title: Run server mode for remote driving
powerMoves.description: Start opencode serve or opencode web and expose it on your network with mDNS if you want other clients to find it.
powerMoves.why: Opens up remote or multi-client workflows instead of tying you to one terminal window.

gotchas.problem: OpenCode config is merged from multiple layers, not replaced.
gotchas.solution: Check remote config, global config, OPENCODE_CONFIG, project opencode.json, .opencode directories, and inline env overrides when behavior seems inconsistent.

gotchas.problem: Legacy keybind/theme settings inside opencode.json are deprecated.
gotchas.solution: Put TUI-specific settings in tui.json or tui.jsonc, not in the runtime config.

gotchas.problem: Undo and redo rely on Git.
gotchas.solution: Make sure the project is a Git repo before expecting /undo and /redo to fully manage file changes.

gotchas.problem: Large MCP catalogs can burn context quickly.
gotchas.solution: Only enable the servers and tool patterns you actually need; GitHub-style MCPs are particularly token-hungry.

gotchas.problem: The default permission model is permissive unless you tighten it.
gotchas.solution: Explicitly set permission rules like bash=ask or edit=ask if you want safer interactive behavior.

gotchas.problem: Desktop app and terminal app share concepts but not every shortcut or UI affordance behaves identically.
gotchas.solution: Treat the desktop app as a richer client, but keep core workflows grounded in agents, config, and commands that also work in the CLI.

cliFlags.description: Execute a one-shot headless task instead of opening the TUI.
cliFlags.example: opencode run "summarize the architecture and key risks"
cliFlags.useCase: Shell automation, scripting, and CI-style use.

cliFlags.description: Attach a headless run to an existing session when supported.
cliFlags.example: opencode run --attach "continue the last task and finish the test fixes"
cliFlags.useCase: Continue an active session from scripts or another terminal.

cliFlags.description: Start the OpenCode server for client/server workflows.
cliFlags.useCase: Remote access, web clients, and multi-device workflows.

cliFlags.description: Run the web UI against the OpenCode server.
cliFlags.useCase: Browser-based interaction instead of terminal-only use.

cliFlags.description: Authenticate with a remote OAuth-enabled MCP server.
cliFlags.useCase: Connecting remote MCP servers that require browser-based auth.

cliFlags.description: List configured MCP servers and their auth status.
cliFlags.useCase: Sanity-checking MCP setup before prompting the model to use those tools.

cliFlags.description: Debug a specific remote MCP server and its OAuth flow.
cliFlags.useCase: Troubleshooting remote MCP connectivity and auth.

slashCommands.description: Add a provider and its API credentials from the TUI.
slashCommands.tip: Best first command on a fresh install if no provider is configured yet.

slashCommands.description: Compact the current session to save context. Alias: /summarize.
slashCommands.tip: Use it proactively on long sessions before context pressure gets ugly.
Pair this with provider config changes to verify the model list you expect is actually loaded.

slashCommands.description: Create or update AGENTS.md for project instructions and context.
slashCommands.tip: OpenCode uses AGENTS.md, not GEMINI.md or CLAUDE.md, as its native project context file.

slashCommands.description: Undo the last message and its file changes.
slashCommands.tip: Works best in a real Git repo because OpenCode uses Git internally for this flow.

slashCommands.description: Redo a previously undone message and changes.
slashCommands.tip: Use immediately after /undo if you only wanted to inspect the prior state.

slashCommands.description: List and switch between sessions. Aliases include /resume and /continue.
slashCommands.tip: Use this instead of keeping many terminal tabs around for separate tasks.
Good for handing context to teammates without copying giant chat logs.

config.recommended: Set an explicit default like anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5 or openai/gpt-5.4 so sessions behave predictably

config.description: Cheaper helper model for lightweight tasks like title generation.
config.recommended: Point this to a cheap fast model from the same provider when possible

config.description: Controls whether tools run freely, ask, or deny; can also be per-command for bash.
config.recommended: Set edit and bash to ask if you want safer default behavior

config.description: Defines local and remote MCP servers, auth, headers, timeouts, and enabled status.
config.recommended: Start with one or two high-value MCP servers and keep enabled=true only where needed

config.description: Global tool enable/disable map, including wildcard patterns for MCP-backed tools.
config.recommended: Disable noisy or dangerous tools globally and selectively re-enable per agent

config.description: Defines custom agents, their prompts, models, tools, permissions, and modes.
config.recommended: Create at least one review-style agent and one planning-focused agent for repeatability

config.description: Selects which primary agent is used when none is specified.
config.recommended: Set to plan if you want a safer read-first default in sensitive repos

config.description: Configures serve/web mode: port, hostname, mDNS, domain, and CORS.
config.recommended: Only bind 0.0.0.0 or enable mDNS when you intentionally want remote discovery/access

proTips: Think of OpenCode as two products sharing one brain: the runtime/server side in opencode.json and the terminal UX side in tui.json.
Use the plan agent first on unfamiliar repos, then switch back to build only when the plan is sound.
Reference files with @ instead of manually pasting code; it is faster and keeps prompts cleaner.
If you rely on /undo, initialize Git before starting a session so you are not surprised later.
Per-agent MCP enablement is the cleanest way to keep heavyweight integrations from polluting every session.
